text.title: Agent in Mob Programming
text.summary: Rules for integrating an LLM agent as a mob participant, with prompt patterns for driver and navigator turns and key gotchas.

rule.statement: Cap mob at 4 humans + 1 agent; beyond that the agent's outputs drown human voices.
rule.rationale: Agent response speed is constant regardless of mob size; in larger mobs it dominates the air time and humans disengage.

rule.statement: Assign a human facilitator who controls the agent's turn — mutes/unmutes it, reviews its prompt, approves its output before the driver acts on it.
rule.rationale: Without a human gatekeeper the agent operates at full speed and the mob degrades to "agent codes, humans approve".

rule.statement: Rotate the agent out of driver every 5–10 minutes on the same timer as humans; log each rotation to MOB_LOG.md.
rule.rationale: Consistent rotation prevents code monoculture and maintains the mob's collaborative character.

rule.statement: Pin the current goal and the last 3 rotation actions in every agent prompt; context resets between rotations.
rule.rationale: Agent loses thread when the driver/navigator prompt switches; MOB_LOG.md re-read on every turn is the fix.

section.title: Prompt Patterns
section.reference: Agent driver prompt: act only on explicit navigator instruction, hand off after 7 minutes.
Agent navigator prompt: propose one next step as intent, no code this rotation.
Append rotation entry and trigger mob.sh handoff.

antipattern.description: Over-fast driver: agent commits before navigators finish discussing.
antipattern.reason: Add an explicit "wait for GO keyword from facilitator" rule in the driver prompt; no commit without GO signal.

antipattern.description: Silent rewrites: agent reformats unrelated code while "fixing one line".
antipattern.reason: Restrict agent edits to changed lines only; facilitator inspects the diff before any commit is allowed.

antipattern.description: Lost context on rotation: switching agent from driver to navigator erases its memory of the last 7 minutes.
antipattern.reason: Maintain MOB_LOG.md and feed it as the first element of every new rotation prompt.

rule.statement: Final push to the remote must be performed by a human; branch protection must block agent-initiated pushes to main.
rule.rationale: Agents cannot be the last line of defense on production code; human sign-off on the commit is non-negotiable.

rule.statement: Schedule one fully-human mob per week to retain the social and mentoring benefits that agents cannot replicate.
rule.rationale: Agent participation cannot substitute for interpersonal trust-building, mentoring dynamics, and tacit knowledge transfer between team members.
